  
**ENVIROBOT**  
**RULEBOOK**

**Environmental Robotics Challenge**

1. **Introduction & Overview**  
   

**1.1  About EnviroBot**

EnviroBot is an inter-team robotics competition that challenges participants to design, build, and operate an autonomous or remote-controlled robot capable of collecting real-time environmental data. The competition focuses on two key environmental parameters — water turbidity and soil moisture — both of which are critical indicators in ecological monitoring and precision agriculture.

This rulebook governs all aspects of the competition, including team eligibility, robot design constraints, arena operations, and judging procedures. All participating teams are required to read, understand, and comply with every rule set out in this document.

**1.2 Competition Objectives**

The primary objectives of the EnviroBot competition are to:

* Promote hands-on learning in robotics, electronics, programming and environmental science.  
* Encourage innovation in sensor integration and data acquisition.  
* Develop skills in engineering design, teamwork, and technical communication.  
* Raise awareness of environmental monitoring through technology.

## 

## 

## **1.3  Key Definitions**

| Term | Definition |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Robot / Bot** | The physical machine designed and operated by a competing team. |
| **Arena** | The official competition surface that contains the designated water and soil zones. |

| Turbidity Sensor | A sensor that measures water clarity by detecting the scattering of light through liquid. |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Capacitive Sensor** | A sensor that measures soil moisture by detecting changes in electrical capacitance. |
| **Measurement Run** | A single timed attempt by a team to collect data across both the water and soil zones. |
| **Control Operator** | The team member who physically operates the robot during a measurement run. |
| **Data Output** | Any display, screen, serial output, or printed/plotted result showing sensor readings. |
| **Reference Value** | The pre-measured environmental value set by judges, used to verify sensor accuracy. |

# 

2. # **Competition Timeline**

| Event | When? | Location |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Opening Day | Wed 10th June, 12:30pm \- 2:30pm | Roundhouse Function Rooms 7 & 8 |
| Workshop 1  (Mech) | Fri 19th June,  6pm \- 8pm | ElecEng G022 |
| Workshop 2  (Elec) | Fri 26th June,  6pm \- 8pm | Elec Eng Lab 201 |
| Workshop 3  (Software) | Fri 3rd July,  6pm \- 8pm | ElecEng G022 |
| Workshop 4  (help session) | Fri 10th July,  6pm \- 8pm | ElecEng G022 |
| Workshop 5  (help session) | Fri 17th July,  6pm \- 8pm | ElecEng G022 |
| Workshop 6  (help session) | Fri 24th July,  6pm \- 8pm | ElecEng G022 |
| Closing Night | Fri 31st July,  5pm \- 9pm | Roundhouse Function Rooms 7 & 8 |

3. #   **Team Requirements**

#  **3.1  Team Composition**

Each competing team must adhere strictly to the following composition rules:

* A team may consist of:  
  * Minimum: 1 participant  
  * Maximum: 6 participants

  Recommended team size: 4-6 participants (1 participant is advised against as the project is very difficult for a single person to complete)

* All team members must be registered prior to the registration deadline (14th June 11:59pm).

**WARNING:** Teams found to have unregistered members participating in any capacity will receive a formal warning. A second offence will result in disqualification.

##   **3.2  Team Registration**

To register for the competition, teams must sign up to the competition on Rubric through the following link: 

[https://campus.hellorubric.com/?eid=62993](https://campus.hellorubric.com/?eid=62993)

Individual signups will be put into teams of 4\. 

##  **3.3  Roles & Responsibilities**

Teams are encouraged to assign distinct roles internally. We recommend at least four members (two mechanical, one electrical, one software).

##   **3.4  Conduct & Eligibility**

* Teams must conduct themselves with professionalism and respect toward other competitors, officials, and equipment.  
* Only registered team members are permitted inside the designated team workspace and pit area.  
* Any form of sabotage, interference with another team's robot, or unsportsmanlike behaviour will result in immediate disqualification.

# 

# 

# 

# 

# 

# 

# 

# 

# 

# 

# **4\.   Robot Specifications**

## **4.1 General Requirements**

Each team must construct a robot capable of:

* Moving around the arena  
* Taking soil moisture measurements  
* Taking water turbidity measurements

The robot may be:

* Remote controlled  
* Semi-autonomous  
* Fully autonomous

## **4.2  Size limit**

The robot must conform to the following maximum physical constraints at the time of the official inspection on Closing Night:

| Dimension / Property | Maximum Allowable Value |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Length** | 400mm |
| **Width** | 300mm |
| **Height** | 400mm |
| **Weight** | 1.5 kg |

* All dimensions must be satisfied with the robot in its starting configuration. Extendable or deployable parts that expand beyond these limits are prohibited.

## **4.3  Provided Components**

Kits will be provided to teams with the following components:

* ESP32 (x1)  
* Water Turbidity Sensor SEN0189 (x1) \- **mandated**  
* Soil Moisture Capacitive Sensor v2.0.0 (x1) \- **mandated**  
* 12V JGA25-370 motors 300RPM (x2)  
* L298N motor driver (x1)  
* MG996R servo (x1)  
* LM2596 buck converter (x1)  
* USB to Micro USB cable (x1)

Competitors **MUST** use the following components in the kit upon signing up to ensure fairness in collecting and reading data:

* Water Turbidity Sensor SEN0189 (Water Zone)  
* Capacitive Soil Moisture Sensor v2.0.0 (Soil Zone)

If found to not be using these components in the robot, this may result in a penalty up to the discretion of the officials and potentially a disqualification.

All other components in the kit are provided but optional to use.

## **4.4  Allowed Components**

Examples include:

* DC motors  
* Gearmotors  
* Servos  
* Batteries  
* Motor drivers  
* Wheels  
* Bearings  
* Structural materials  
* 3D printed parts  
* Laser-cut parts

Additional sensors may be used unless otherwise specified.

## **4.5 Restricted Components**

The following are prohibited:

* Combustion engines  
* Mains-powered systems  
* Pressurised gas systems  
* Pyrotechnics  
* Dangerous chemicals  
* Open flames  
* Devices intended to damage other robots

Organisers may prohibit any component considered unsafe.

##   **4.6  Data Output & Display**

Teams must implement a method to display or output sensor data in real time or upon completion of a measurement run. Acceptable data output formats include:

* Serial monitor output visible on a connected laptop or device.  
* Live table plotted on a connected computer (e.g. via Arduino Serial Plotter, Python matplotlib, or similar).  
* Printed data log or CSV file output reviewed immediately after the run.

Teams wishing to use an alternative output method not listed above must seek prior written approval from the Project Directors. 

# **5\.   Arena & Competition Specifics**

## **5.1  Arena Description**

![][image1]

The arena is an octagonal wooden structure with a diameter of \~1.5 m. It is divided into four terrain sectors of increasing difficulty, which may include surfaces such as sandpaper, grass, wet soil, and/or sand (examples only). These terrains are designed to simulate different ground conditions and challenge the robot's navigation capabilities.

Each sector contains two soil zones and one water zone, providing designated sampling points for environmental data collection. Teams are expected to collect data from all of the zones within the arena.

## **5.2  Competition Format**

The competition part of EnviroBot will be run across two days, Thursday and Friday of Week 9\. 

**Day 1 (Thursday W9) is the Testing Day**, focused entirely on the robots. Teams will bring their robots to the arena, pass technical inspection, complete their measurement run, and have their data and robot assessed by the judging panel.

 **Day 2 (Friday W9) is the official Closing Night**. Teams present their findings and environmental conclusions to the judges in a structured case competition.

Details are subject to change as we get closer to Closing Night. 

### **5.2.1  Day 1 \- Testing Day** 

**5.2.1.1 Registration & Technical Inspection**

All teams must report to the inspection station at their allocated time. During inspection, officials will verify:

* Robot dimensions conform to Section 3.1.  
* Both required sensors are present and functional.  
* Battery safety rules (Section 3.5.2) are met.  
* A data output method is demonstrated by producing at least one reading.  
* No prohibited components are fitted (Section 3.6).

Inspection is pass/fail. Teams that fail inspection have 30 minutes to rectify issues before forfeiting their spot.

**5.2.1.2 Measurement Run**

Each team will be allocated a timed measurement run on the arena. The following conditions apply:

* Maximum run duration: 8 minutes (unless otherwise stated in the Event Briefing).  
* The control operator must remain within the designated operator zone throughout the run.  
* The robot must navigate to each of the four zones in the arena each with a different terrain. As stated in Section 5.1, each terrain will contain soil and water pots with varying levels of moisture and turbidity respectively. Teams must aim to collect readings from all of these pots within the allocated time frame of 8 minutes  
* The readings must be captured and available for judge review immediately after the run.  
* The run ends when the team signals completion, the timer expires, or the robot becomes inoperable.  
* If the robot gets stuck, the officials will move the robot into the centre of the arena to try again. This will be done as many times until the run duration is out.

**NOTE**: Teams are responsible for resetting their robot and data output system to a ready state before their allocated run slot. Delays due to team unpreparedness will be counted against run time.

**5.2.1.3 Data Review and Robot Assessment**

Immediately following the measurement run, the team will present their recorded data to the judging panel. The panel will compare the robot's readings against the pre-set reference values. Teams must be able to:

* Show the raw sensor output or processed readings clearly.  
* Demonstrate a plotted graph or data log if applicable.  
* Answer basic technical questions about data acquisition methodology

Following this, there will be an assessment of the robot where the officials will judge on Categories 3, 4 and 5 detailed in Section 6\. A 3 minute presentation will be required for teams to discuss any innovative features of their robot, the build quality as well as the aesthetics. The officials may also ask questions which teams will be expected to answer.

**5.2.1.4  Scoring Rounds**

* Each team completes one (1) official measurement run unless the Event Briefing specifies multiple rounds.  
* If multiple rounds are held, the best single run score will be used unless otherwise stated.  
* Teams that are unable to complete a run due to technical failure will receive zero points for Day 1 criteria only; other judging criteria remain scoreable.

### **5.2.2  Day 2 \- Closing Night**

**5.2.2.1  Case Competition**

Closing Night centres on a case competition in which teams are given a problem scenario and one hour to develop and prepare a response using the sensor data they collected during Day 1 \- Testing Day. The problem scenario will be revealed at the start of Closing Night.

Teams are expected to build a genuine argument from their data, acknowledge tradeoffs, and defend their recommendations under questioning. The problem scenarios are designed to have no single correct answer. The quality of reasoning and how well it is grounded in the collected data is what will be judged.

Each team will then deliver a structured verbal presentation of up to 3 minutes followed by a Q\&A with the judging panel. Further details on presentation requirements will be communicated closer to the event.

### **5.2.3  Tie-Breaking**

In the event of tied total scores, the following criteria will be applied in order:

1. Higher score in Category 1: Technical Measurement Performance.  
2. Higher score in Category 2: Data Output & Visualisation.  
3. Higher score in Category 6: Verbal Presentation.  
4. If still tied, the competition officials will make a final decision.

# 

# **6\.   Judging Criteria**

## **6.1  Scoring Overview**

The competition is scored out of a maximum of 100 points across seven (7) judging categories. Each category is independently scored by the judging panel. The table below summarises all categories and their weightings:

 

| \# | Judging Category | Sub-Criteria | Max Points |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | :---: |
| 1 | **Technical Measurement Performance** | Sensor readings attempted, accuracy vs reference, both sensors active | 30 |
| 2 | **Data Output & Visualisation** | Correct data display / graph plotted, labelling, units, readability | 10 |
| 3 | **Build Quality & Workmanship** | Structural integrity, neat wiring, component mounting, robustness | 5 |
| 4 | **Innovation & Engineering Design** | Novelty of approach, unique features, creative solutions | 10 |
| 5 | **Robot Aesthetics** | Overall finish, visual appeal, team branding / labelling | 5 |
| 6 | **Closing Night Case Competition Presentation** | Clarity, understanding of system, Q\&A response quality | 40 |
|   | **TOTAL** |   | **100** |

 

**6.2  Category Descriptions**

### **6.2.1  Technical Measurement Performance  (40 pts)**

This is the highest-weighted category and evaluates whether the robot actually performs its core purpose: collecting environmental measurements.

| Points | Performance Descriptor |
| :---- | :---- |
| **23 – 25** | Both sensors produce accurate readings within ±10% of the reference value. Robot navigates to both zones independently and completes the full run. |
| **18 – 22** | Both sensors are active and produce readings; one reading is within ±10% of reference. Minor navigation assistance required. |
| **12 – 17** | Only one sensor produces a valid reading, or both readings deviate more than ±10% from reference. |
| **5 – 11** | Sensors are connected but produce unreliable or inconsistent readings. Significant operator intervention required. |
| **0 – 4** | Robot fails to take any valid measurement during the run. |

### **6.2.2  Data Output & Visualisation  (10 pts)**

This category evaluates the quality and clarity of the data output produced by the robot system.

*  Is the output legible and clearly labelled with appropriate units (e.g. NTU for turbidity, % for moisture)?  
* Is a graph or data log produced and is it correctly formatted (axes labelled, title present, appropriate scale)?  
* Is the data output available promptly after the run for judge review?  
*  Does the visualisation aid interpretation of the environmental conditions measured?

### **6.2.3  Build Quality & Workmanship  (10 pts)**

* Judges will evaluate the physical construction of the robot.  
* Structural integrity — does the robot hold together under normal operating conditions?  
* Wiring neatness — are cables exposed and routed safely away from moving parts?  
* Component mounting — are sensors, boards, and modules firmly fixed and appropriately positioned?  
* Maneuverability \- Can the bot navigate around different terrain without getting stuck?  
* Safety compliance — are all exposed connections insulated and all mandatory safety rules met?

### **6.2.4  Innovation & Engineering Design  (10 pts)**

* This category rewards creativity and original thinking in the robot's design.  
* Has the team implemented a novel solution to a design challenge (e.g. auto-deployment probe, multi-sample averaging, waterproof enclosure design)?  
*  Is the sensor integration approach more sophisticated than a basic wired connection?  
* Has the team gone beyond the minimum specification to improve performance or usability?  
*  Is there evidence of iterative design thinking (prototyping, testing, refinement)?

### **6.2.5  Robot Aesthetics (5 pts)**

While function takes priority, the visual finish of the robot contributes to this score.

* Overall appearance — is the robot tidy and well-finished?  
* Are components arranged logically and attractively, or is the design haphazard in appearance?  
* Does the robot convey a sense of professional craftsmanship?

### **6.2.6  Closing Night Case Competition Presentation (25 pts)**

* This category assesses the team's ability to analyse their environmental data and develop a well-reasoned response to the assigned problem scenario.  
* Argument quality: are recommendations logical, specific, and genuinely derived from the collected data?  
* Tradeoff handling: does the team acknowledge limitations, competing priorities, and uncertainty in their data?  
* Q\&A performance: can the team defend their reasoning and answer probing questions without relying on prepared material?   
* Teamwork: do multiple members contribute meaningfully?  
* Clarity: is the presentation coherent and easy to follow?

## **6.3  Deductions**

The following deductions may be applied by the judging panel in addition to scoring:

 

| Infraction | Deduction |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Use of prohibited components** | –10 pts or disqualification |
| **Run exceeds maximum time** | –5 pts (run continues but capped) |
| **Pre-loaded / hardcoded sensor data** | Category 1 & 2 zeroed out |
| **Damage to arena surface** | –5 pts per incident |

# 

# **7\.   Safety & General Regulations**

## **7.1  General Safety Requirements**

**WARNING:** Safety rules are non-negotiable. Violations may result in the robot being immediately removed from competition regardless of progress in the event.

* All team members must wear enclosed footwear at all times within the competition venue.  
* No soldering, grinding, or cutting is permitted within the competition area. All such work must occur in designated workshop areas only.  
* Spills of water or other liquids must be reported to officials immediately.  
* The competition officials reserve the right to stop any run deemed to present a safety hazard.

## **7.2  Electrical & Battery Safety**

In addition to the mandatory battery rules in Section 3.5.2:

*  Teams must be able to immediately power down their robot upon instruction from any official.  
* Any robot emitting smoke, sparks, or unusual heat must be powered down immediately and reported to officials.  
* No modifications to the robot's electrical system are permitted once the measurement run has commenced.

## **7.3  Arena Conduct**

Teams may not enter the arena surface during a competitor's active run.

Teams must retrieve their robot promptly at the end of their allocated run slot.

Any robot that has become inoperable in the arena must be retrieved manually by the operator and placed outside.

 Officials may intervene to remove a robot that presents a safety hazard.

## **7.4  Intellectual Property & Academic Integrity**

* All design and construction work must be completed by registered team members only.  
*  Open-source libraries, purchased modules, and commercially available components are permitted and encouraged.  
*  Teams must be able to explain the purpose and functionality of every component on their robot. Inability to do so may reduce scores in the relevant judging categories.  
* Plagiarism of another team's design — including copying circuit schematics or code without acknowledgement — is grounds for disqualification.

## **7.5  Disqualification Conditions**

* A team may be immediately disqualified for any of the following:  
* Deliberate sabotage or interference with another team's robot or equipment.  
* Presenting hardcoded or fabricated sensor data as live readings.  
* Failure to comply with a safety instruction issued by an official.  
* Abusive, threatening, or disrespectful behaviour toward any official, competitor, or bystander.  
* Possession of prohibited components on the robot during an official run.

# 

# **8\.   Amendments Notice**

## **8.1  Rule Amendments**

# The organising committee reserves the right to amend these rules at any time prior to the competition. Any amendments will be communicated to all registered teams in writing and a revised version of this rulebook will be issued. Teams are responsible for ensuring they are working from the most current version.
